optimist.name: Marlene Holmqvist
optimist.column:
The Twins took a step forward yesterday, and that’s the kind of progress that whispers, “This is the year.” Eight runs, a solid pitching performance, and a reminder that even when the standings don’t tell the whole story, the game is still full of possibilities. Sure, the AL Central still has them two games back, but that’s not the point. The point is that the Twins are playing like a team that believes in the script written by the universe—specifically, the one where they’re not just surviving but thriving.  

Buxton’s knees feel good this week and that’s all the forecast I need. Minnesota nice is about to turn into Minnesota nasty, mark it down. The way he’s swinging the bat, the way he’s diving for catches, it’s like the heavens themselves are saying, “Hey, remember when you thought this team was done?” The kid’s got fire in his step, and that fire’s contagious. You can see it in the way the outfielders are chasing fly balls like they’re the last hope of humanity. You can hear it in the crowd when they’re chanting for the starters to keep going, even when the count is full.  

The pitching staff? They’re not perfect, but they

pessimist.name: Gunnar 'Gunny' Bjornstad
pessimist.column:
The Twins won again. That’s the headline. The Blue Jays lost again. That’s the story. Minnesota 8, Toronto 2. A blowout, a statement, a reminder that the AL Central is a brutal division and the Twins are still floundering in the middle of it. Uff da, again.  

But here’s the thing: this win felt like a mirage. A temporary fix for a leaky boat. The Twins scored eight runs, sure, but it was the same old script—some clutch hitting, some shaky pitching, and a bullpen that’s still trying to figure out how to keep a game close. The offense looked alive, but only for a few innings. The pitching staff? Well, they’re still the same pitching staff that’s been struggling to find consistency since the rebuild started.
